meta.note: Every patient, clinician, result, and note in this file is fictitious and generated for a hackathon demo.

patients.name: Margaret Holloway

depthPack.placeholder: Synthetic diabetes/CKD sample used to exercise R-09. It is not the pending vaginal-cancer evidence pack.
depthPack.trigger: The synthetic plan reinforces diet but names no nutrition owner or follow-up action.
depthPack.headline: A1c rose to 8.4: The plan names no nutrition owner.
depthPack.gap: The plan reinforces diet but does not document a nutrition owner, dietary intake, patient preference, or access. Consider whether assessment or referral belongs in the plan; the patient and clinician decide.
depthPack.question: Is there enough chart evidence to refer now, or should the missing intake, preference, and access data be collected first?

research.title: ADA–KDIGO consensus report: Diabetes management in chronic kidney disease (2022)
research.note: The report supports comprehensive care built on healthy lifestyle; it does not determine referral timing for this synthetic patient.

specialists.name: A. Boyd, MD — nephrology clinic RD team

depthPack.quickScripted: Underdetermined: The chart supports discussing structured nutrition follow-through, but it lacks a dietary recall, the patient's preference, access constraints, and a documented referral goal. Collect those inputs or discuss a referral now; this scripted lane does not choose the timing. The patient and clinician decide.
depthPack.followUpQuestion: A synthetic nutrition referral now has an owner and due date. What must follow-up verify before any patient-specific nutrition plan changes?
depthPack.followUpQuickScripted: The simulated referral creates ownership, not clinical closure. Follow-up still needs dietary intake, the patient's goals and preferences, access confirmation, and any kidney-specific nutrition constraints. No patient-specific nutrition plan should change until those inputs are reviewed by the patient and clinician.

meds: Acetaminophen 500 mg PO PRN knee pain

priorNotes.author: RIVERA.A
priorNotes.preview: T2DM follow-up. A1c 7.9, trending up. Reinforced diet, continued metformin. Plan: recheck A1c in 3 months; consider adding second agent (SGLT2i) if above goal, given CKD 3a.

priorNotes.author: NGO.T (RN)
priorNotes.preview: Patient called re: knee pain after walking program. Advised acetaminophen, avoid NSAIDs given CKD. Will discuss at next visit.

note.hpi: Ms. Holloway is a 72-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes, hypertension, and stage 3a chronic kidney disease who returns for scheduled diabetes follow-up. Since April she reports good adherence to metformin but more frequent evening snacking. Home fasting glucose runs 150–190 mg/dL. No polyuria, polydipsia, blurred vision, chest pain, or hypoglycemic episodes. She walks about 20 minutes most days; knee pain is controlled with acetaminophen. No numbness, tingling, or foot lesions.
note.ros: Constitutional: no weight loss, fevers, or fatigue. Cardiovascular: no chest pain, palpitations, orthopnea, or edema. Neurologic: no paresthesias. All other systems reviewed and negative.
note.exam: BP 142/84, HR 76, BMI 29.4. Well-appearing, in no distress. Heart: regular rate and rhythm, no murmurs. Lungs: clear to auscultation. Extremities: no edema; monofilament sensation intact bilaterally; skin of both feet without lesions.
ap: Type 2 diabetes, above goal (HbA1c 8.4%, up from 7.9%): reinforced dietary changes with focus on evening snacking. Continue metformin 1,000 mg BID. Start empagliflozin 10 mg daily given CKD stage 3a and cardiovascular risk; counseled on genital mycotic infections and volume symptoms. Repeat HbA1c and BMP in 3 months.
CKD stage 3a with albuminuria (eGFR 52, UACR 64): continue lisinopril. Recheck BMP and urine albumin/creatinine in 3 months. Continue to avoid NSAIDs.
Hypertension, above goal today (142/84): home BP log for 2 weeks; if persistently above 130/80, will increase lisinopril to 40 mg daily.
Preventive: pneumococcal vaccine due — ordered today. Diabetic eye exam scheduled with ophthalmology. Flu vaccine in the fall.

qa.a: Her HbA1c on 2026-07-02 was 8.4% (high), up from 7.9% on 2026-03-14. In today's visit she reported home fasting readings of 150–190 mg/dL, which is consistent with that trend. The drafted plan adds empagliflozin and repeats the HbA1c in 3 months.

With an eGFR of 52, albuminuria, and type 2 diabetes above goal, adding an SGLT2 inhibitor aligns with current ADA/KDIGO direction for combined glycemic, kidney, and cardiovascular benefit. Typical initiation is empagliflozin 10 mg daily with counseling on mycotic infections and volume status. (Synthetic demo summary — verify against your institution's formulary and current guidance.)

Metformin is generally continued at full dose when eGFR is 45 or above. Her eGFR is 52, so current dosing is acceptable with renal function rechecked at least every 6 months, and sooner given the new SGLT2 inhibitor. (Synthetic demo summary.)

patients.aiFallback: Grounded in this visit and the synthetic chart: Ms. Holloway's central issue today is diabetes above goal (HbA1c 8.4%) in the setting of CKD 3a with albuminuria and borderline blood pressure. The drafted plan adds empagliflozin, keeps metformin, and tightens BP follow-up. You can ask me about her A1c trend, the SGLT2 rationale, or metformin renal dosing.

patients.name: James Okafor

priorNotes.author: RIVERA.A
priorNotes.preview: New paroxysmal AF confirmed on 14-day monitor. CHA2DS2-VASc 3 (HTN, prior TIA). Started apixaban 5 mg BID and metoprolol. Cardiology referral placed.

note.cc: Follow-up of paroxysmal atrial fibrillation and palpitations.
note.hpi: Mr. Okafor is a 58-year-old man with paroxysmal atrial fibrillation (CHA2DS2-VASc 3: hypertension, prior TIA), on apixaban and metoprolol, returning for follow-up. Palpitation episodes are down from several per week to roughly one brief episode per week, without syncope, chest pain, or dyspnea. He admits missing apixaban doses roughly twice a month while traveling for work. Exercise tolerance is stable; he climbs three flights without symptoms.
note.ros: Cardiovascular: intermittent palpitations as above; no chest pain, presyncope, or edema. Neurologic: no focal weakness, speech change, or visual loss since 2024 TIA. All other systems reviewed and negative.
note.exam: BP 128/79, HR 72, irregularly irregular rhythm. No murmurs or gallops. Lungs clear. No jugular venous distention. No peripheral edema.
ap: Paroxysmal atrial fibrillation: symptoms improved on metoprolol succinate 50 mg daily; continue current dose. Rate adequately controlled today.

Stroke prevention (CHA2DS2-VASc 3): continue apixaban 5 mg BID — no dose-reduction criteria met. Discussed the risk of missed doses given prior TIA; patient will use a travel pill organizer and phone reminders.
Hypertension: at goal today on amlodipine and metoprolol; continue current regimen.
Obesity: discussed weight goals; offered nutrition referral — patient will consider and decide at next visit.
Follow-up: cardiology visit already scheduled in August; repeat visit here in 3 months or sooner for new symptoms.

qa.a: Apixaban 5 mg BID is the appropriate dose here: he meets no dose-reduction criteria (under 80, over 60 kg, creatinine 1.02). The visit surfaced missed doses about twice monthly while traveling — notable given his prior TIA — and the plan documents a pill organizer plus phone reminders. (Synthetic demo summary.)
His documented CHA2DS2-VASc score is 3 (hypertension, prior TIA counting 2 points), which supports ongoing anticoagulation. This is recorded in the May 2026 progress note. (Synthetic demo summary.)

He is currently on a rate-control strategy with metoprolol, with symptoms improving. Rhythm-control options (including ablation) are on the cardiology agenda for his August consult; nothing in today's synthetic visit forces that decision earlier.

patients.aiFallback: Grounded in this visit and the synthetic chart: Mr. Okafor's AF symptoms are improving on rate control, and the key safety thread is anticoagulation adherence while traveling, given his prior TIA. Ask me about his apixaban dosing, stroke-risk score, or the rate-versus-rhythm plan.

patients.name: Rosa Delgado

priorNotes.author: RIVERA.A
priorNotes.preview: COPD confirmed on spirometry (FEV1 58%, ratio 0.61). One moderate exacerbation this spring, treated outpatient. Smoking down from 20 to ~5 cigarettes/day. Started tiotropium.

note.cc: COPD follow-up with persistent exertional dyspnea.
note.hpi: Ms. Delgado is a 66-year-old woman with GOLD 2 group B COPD and a 40 pack-year history, now smoking about 5 cigarettes daily (down from 20). On tiotropium since May she still becomes short of breath after two flights of stairs and uses albuterol about three times per week. One moderate exacerbation this spring treated as an outpatient; none since. No orthopnea, chest pain, hemoptysis, or weight loss. She is interested in quitting smoking completely and asks about medication support.
note.ros: Respiratory: exertional dyspnea and rare morning cough, minimal sputum. Cardiovascular: no chest pain or edema. Constitutional: no weight loss or night sweats. All other systems reviewed and negative.
note.exam: BP 134/80, HR 82, SpO2 94% on room air. Prolonged expiratory phase with scattered end-expiratory wheezes, no crackles. Heart regular without murmurs. No cyanosis, clubbing, or edema.
ap: COPD, GOLD 2 group B, symptomatic on LAMA alone: step up to combination LABA/LAMA inhaler (tiotropium/olodaterol) replacing tiotropium monotherapy. Inhaler technique reviewed with teach-back today. Continue albuterol PRN.

Tobacco use disorder: patient motivated to quit — started varenicline with standard titration and set a quit date of August 1. Quitline referral placed. Discussed that blood eosinophils (250) and any further exacerbations would prompt reassessment for an ICS-containing regimen.
Hypertension: at goal; continue hydrochlorothiazide.
Preventive: RSV vaccine and updated pneumococcal vaccine due — both ordered today. Low-dose CT lung cancer screening discussed and scheduled (age and pack-year criteria met).
Follow-up: 6 weeks to review symptoms on the new inhaler and quit progress.

qa.a: She remains symptomatic (mMRC-level exertional dyspnea, albuterol 3×/week) on LAMA monotherapy, so today's plan steps up to a LABA/LAMA combination, which is the usual next move for group B disease. Technique was re-taught with teach-back. (Synthetic demo summary — confirm against current GOLD guidance.)

She is down to ~5 cigarettes/day and asked for help quitting. The plan starts varenicline with a quit date of August 1 plus quitline support — the combination of pharmacotherapy and counseling has the strongest quit evidence. (Synthetic demo summary.)

Her blood eosinophil count is 250 cells/µL with one moderate exacerbation this year. Today's plan stays with LABA/LAMA, documenting that another exacerbation — especially with eosinophils in this range — would prompt consideration of an ICS-containing regimen. (Synthetic demo summary.)

patients.aiFallback: Grounded in this visit and the synthetic chart: Ms. Delgado is still symptomatic on tiotropium alone, so the draft steps up to LABA/LAMA, starts varenicline with an August 1 quit date, and orders due vaccines plus lung cancer screening. Ask me about the inhaler step-up, cessation plan, or when an ICS would come into play.

patients.name: Ethan Park

priorNotes.author: RIVERA.A
priorNotes.preview: Asthma stable over winter with rare albuterol use. GAD well controlled on sertraline 50 mg. Refills provided.

note.hpi: Mr. Park is a 33-year-old man with asthma and generalized anxiety disorder presenting for refills and asthma review. Since spring his albuterol use has increased to about three times per week with occasional nighttime symptoms roughly once a month, worse with pollen and exercise. No urgent visits or oral steroids. Anxiety remains well controlled on sertraline 50 mg; sleep is good apart from the asthma awakenings; no medication side effects.
note.ros: Respiratory: intermittent wheeze and chest tightness as above; no fevers or productive cough. Psychiatric: mood stable, no anhedonia, no suicidal ideation. All other systems reviewed and negative.
note.exam: BP 118/72, HR 68. Comfortable, speaking in full sentences. Lungs: clear at rest, no wheezes today. Heart regular. No accessory muscle use.
ap: Asthma, now partly controlled (reliever use 3×/week, monthly nocturnal symptoms): switch from albuterol-only to as-needed budesonide–formoterol (anti-inflammatory reliever approach). Reviewed technique and a written action plan; albuterol may be finished as backup.
Generalized anxiety disorder: stable on sertraline 50 mg daily — continue; refill sent. Reinforced existing CBT skills; no dose change.
Preventive: influenza vaccine in the fall; no other gaps identified.
Follow-up: 8 weeks to reassess asthma control (ACT score), sooner for any exacerbation.

qa.a: His reliever use (3×/week with monthly night symptoms) signals partial control on albuterol alone. The draft moves him to as-needed ICS–formoterol, the anti-inflammatory reliever strategy modern asthma guidance prefers at this step, and includes an updated action plan. (Synthetic demo summary — confirm against current GINA guidance.)

His GAD is stable on sertraline 50 mg with no side effects and good sleep apart from asthma awakenings, so the plan continues the current dose with refills — no escalation indicated in this synthetic visit.

patients.aiFallback: Grounded in this visit and the synthetic chart: Mr. Park's asthma has drifted to partly controlled, so the draft switches him to as-needed budesonide–formoterol with an action plan, while his anxiety stays stable on sertraline. Ask me about the reliever switch or his anxiety management.

patients.name: Priya Natarajan
problems: Family history of premature CAD — father, MI at 52 (Z82.49)

priorNotes.author: RIVERA.A
priorNotes.preview: Annual exam. A1c 5.8 — prediabetes counseling. TSH in range on levothyroxine 75. Cervical screening performed, results normal. Mammogram ordered.

note.hpi: Ms. Natarajan is a 44-year-old woman with prediabetes, treated hypothyroidism, hyperlipidemia, and a family history of premature coronary disease (father, MI at 52), here for her annual physical. She feels well. She exercises twice weekly and has held her weight steady. No chest pain, palpitations, cold intolerance, or menstrual changes. She asks what screening will become due when she turns 45 on November 2.
note.ros: Complete review of systems performed and negative in detail, including constitutional, cardiovascular, endocrine, and gastrointestinal systems.
note.exam: BP 122/76, HR 70, BMI 26.2. General, cardiac, pulmonary, abdominal, thyroid, skin, and lymphatic examinations normal. No thyromegaly or nodules.
ap: Prediabetes (HbA1c 5.9, was 5.8): continue lifestyle focus — offered structured Diabetes Prevention Program referral; repeat HbA1c in 6 months.

Hyperlipidemia with family history of premature CAD (LDL 128): the displayed synthetic chart does not document every input needed to reproduce a 10-year ASCVD estimate, so no exact percentage is asserted. Continue lifestyle work, verify the complete risk inputs, and then use shared decision-making about statin therapy.
Hypothyroidism: TSH 3.4 in range — continue levothyroxine 75 mcg daily; annual TSH.

Preventive, currently age 44: average-risk colorectal cancer screening begins at 45. Discussed colonoscopy and stool-based options now; plan to initiate screening on or after her November 2 birthday unless an earlier indication emerges. Mammogram scheduled. Cervical screening up to date (2025). Dental and eye reminders given.
Follow-up: 6 months for lipid and A1c recheck; annual physical in 12 months.

qa.a: Average-risk colorectal cancer screening begins at age 45. She is 44 on the demo date and turns 45 on November 2, so the synthetic plan discusses options now and starts screening on or after that birthday unless an earlier indication emerges. (Synthetic demo summary — confirm against current guidance.)

Her LDL is 128 and her father had an MI at 52, but the displayed synthetic chart does not document every input needed to reproduce an exact 10-year ASCVD estimate. The safe next step is to verify the complete inputs and use shared decision-making rather than present an unsupported percentage. (Synthetic demo summary.)
TSH is 3.4 mIU/L — within range on levothyroxine 75 mcg — so the plan continues the current dose with annual monitoring.

patients.aiFallback: In this scripted synthetic visit, Ms. Natarajan is 44, has LDL 128 with a family history of premature coronary disease, and turns 45 on November 2. The draft verifies complete cardiovascular-risk inputs before giving a percentage and plans colorectal screening on or after her birthday. Ask about the screening timeline, risk discussion, or A1c plan.
